title: The Imperfect (Pretérito Imperfecto)
description: Learn the imperfect tense: regular -AR endings (-aba) and -ER/-IR endings (-ía), the only three irregulars (ser, ir, ver), and its core uses — habitual past ('used to / would'), descriptions and background, age, time and weather in the past, and ongoing actions.

cards.front: hablaba, hablabas, hablaba, hablábamos, hablabais, hablaban
cards.notes: Regular **-AR** imperfect endings: **-aba, -abas, -aba, -ábamos, -abais, -aban**. Only the *nosotros* form carries a written accent: *hablábamos*. Note that *yo* and *él/ella* are identical: *hablaba*.

cards.front: comía, comías, comía, comíamos, comíais, comían
cards.back: imperfect of -ER and -IR verbs (comer = to eat)
cards.notes: **-ER** and **-IR** verbs share one set of endings: **-ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían**. Every form carries an accent on the **í**. As with -AR, *yo* and *él/ella* match: *comía*. So *comer* and *vivir* behave the same: *comía / vivía*.

cards.front: era, eras, era, éramos, erais, eran
cards.notes: *ser* is one of only **three** irregular verbs in the imperfect. *Era médico* = I was a doctor; *Era alto* = he was tall. ⚠️ The imperfect of *ser* is **era**, NOT *fue* (that is the preterite). Use *era* for descriptions and background in the past.

cards.front: iba, ibas, iba, íbamos, ibais, iban
cards.notes: *ir* is irregular in the imperfect: **iba, ibas, iba, íbamos, ibais, iban**. Only *íbamos* has an accent. *Iba al colegio en autobús* = I used to go to school by bus.

cards.front: veía, veías, veía, veíamos, veíais, veían
cards.back: imperfect of VER (to see / to watch) — irregular
cards.notes: *ver* is irregular only because it keeps the **e** of the stem: **veía, veías, veía, veíamos, veíais, veían** (the endings are otherwise the regular -ER/-IR ones). *Veía la tele cada noche* = I used to watch TV every night.

cards.front: De niño, jugaba al fútbol todos los días.
cards.notes: The imperfect expresses **repeated or habitual** past actions. Both English *used to* AND *would* (habitually) map to the imperfect: *jugaba* = I used to play / I would play. ⚠️ Don't be fooled by *would* — here it is NOT a conditional, it is a habit in the past.

cards.front: La casa era grande y tenía un jardín.
cards.back: descriptions and background in the past
cards.notes: Use the imperfect to **describe** the past: scenery, people, emotions, the 'background' of a story. *Era grande* (it was big), *tenía un jardín* (it had a garden). These set the scene rather than report a single completed event.

cards.front: Tenía ocho años. Eran las tres. Llovía.
cards.back: age, time (clock), and weather in the past
cards.notes: The imperfect is the default for **age** (*tenía ocho años* = I was eight), **clock time** (*eran las tres* = it was three o'clock — note plural *eran*), and **weather** (*llovía*, *hacía frío*). For 'it was one o'clock' use the singular: *era la una*.

The imperfect describes an action **in progress** in the past — often translated with *was/were -ing*: *estudiaba* = I was studying. *Mientras* (while) frequently links two ongoing actions: *Mientras cocinaba, cantaba.*

cards.front: siempre, todos los días, antes, mientras, cuando era niño/a, generalmente
cards.back: time markers that signal the imperfect
cards.notes: Words that point to habit, repetition or background trigger the imperfect: **siempre** (always), **todos los días** (every day), **antes** (before/in the past), **mientras** (while), **cuando era niño/a** (when I was a child), **generalmente** (usually). Compare with the preterite's *ayer, anoche* (lesson 03).

cards.front: Era feliz.  ✗ Fue feliz (for a lasting state)
cards.back: use ERA (imperfect) for past states, not FUE
cards.notes: For a **lasting state or description** in the past, use the imperfect *era*: *Cuando era joven, era feliz* = When I was young, I was happy. *fue* is the preterite (a completed event) and belongs to lesson 03. ⚠️ Common error: saying *fue* where a description needs *era*.

cards.back: the imperfect has NO stem changes
cards.notes: Unlike the present or preterite, the imperfect has **no** stem changes and no spelling tricks (apart from the 3 irregulars). *dormir* → *dormía* (not *durmía*), *pedir* → *pedía*, *jugar* → *jugaba*, *querer* → *quería*. Just take the stem and add the endings.

steps.title: The past you describe and repeat
steps.body:
# The Imperfect (Pretérito Imperfecto)

Spanish has two simple past tenses. The **preterite** (lesson 01) reports single, completed events. The **imperfect** is for the past you *describe* and *repeat*: habits, background, ongoing scenes. (How they contrast comes in lesson 03 — here we focus on the imperfect alone.)

The two regular patterns:

| | -AR (*hablar*) | -ER/-IR (*comer / vivir*) |
|---|---|---|
| yo | hablaba | comía |
| tú | hablabas | comías |
| él/ella | hablaba | comía |
| nosotros | hablábamos | comíamos |
| vosotros | hablabais | comíais |
| ellos | hablaban | comían |

**-ER and -IR share one set of endings** (*-ía...*). Notice that **yo and él/ella are identical** in both columns (*hablaba / comía*) — context or a subject pronoun tells them apart.

> ✅ **No stem changes here.** Forget the tricks from the present and preterite: *dormir* → *dormía*, *pedir* → *pedía*, *jugar* → *jugaba*. Just stem + ending.

steps.title: Only three irregulars
steps.body:
## The good news: just three irregular verbs

The imperfect is the most regular tense in Spanish. There are **only three** irregular verbs — *ser*, *ir*, and *ver*:

| | ser (to be) | ir (to go) | ver (to see) |
|---|---|---|---|
| yo | era | iba | veía |
| tú | eras | ibas | veías |
| él/ella | era | iba | veía |
| nosotros | éramos | íbamos | veíamos |
| vosotros | erais | ibais | veíais |
| ellos | eran | iban | veían |

Notice *ver* is barely irregular: it simply keeps the **e** (*ve-ía*) and then uses the normal -ER endings.

> ⚠️ **era, not fue.** The imperfect of *ser* is **era**. *Fue* is the *preterite*. For descriptions and lasting states in the past — *Era alto, era simpático* — you want **era**. Saying *fue alto* for a description is a classic English-speaker error.

steps.title: When to use the imperfect
steps.body:
## Four main uses

**1. Habitual / repeated past — 'used to' and 'would'.** Things you did regularly:

- *De niño, jugaba al fútbol todos los días.* = As a child, I used to play / would play football every day.

> ⚠️ English *would* is tricky. *I would play* (a habit) = imperfect. This is **not** the conditional. *Used to* and habitual *would* both become the imperfect.

**2. Descriptions and background.** Setting the scene — people, places, emotions:

- *La casa era grande y tenía un jardín.* = The house was big and had a garden.

**3. Age, clock time, and weather in the past:**

- *Tenía ocho años.* = I was eight. | *Eran las tres.* = It was three o'clock. | *Llovía.* = It was raining.

**4. Ongoing action ('was -ing'):**

- *Mientras estudiaba, escuchaba música.* = While I was studying, I was listening to music.

**Time markers** that signal the imperfect: *siempre, todos los días, antes, mientras, cuando era niño/a, generalmente.*

exercise.prompt: Match each subject with the correct imperfect form of *hablar* (to speak).
Translate: *I used to eat a lot.* (comer)
exercise.hint: 'used to eat' is a past habit → imperfect: comer → comía (yo).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *She was eight years old.* (use tener for age)
exercise.hint: Spanish gives age with tener → tenía (imperfect for age in the past).

exercise.prompt: A friend describes childhood: 'Every summer we would go to the beach.' Which sentence is correct? (habitual 'would' = imperfect)

images.label: Todos los veranos íbamos a la playa.
Todos los veranos fuimos a la playa.
Todos los veranos iríamos a la playa.
Todos los veranos vamos a la playa.

exercise.hint: Habitual 'would' in the past = imperfect, not the conditional (iríamos) or preterite (fuimos).
distractors: Todos los veranos fuimos a la playa.
Todos los veranos iríamos a la playa.
Todos los veranos vamos a la playa.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imperfect of *ser* (él/ella) to describe the house.
exercise.hint: Description in the past → imperfect of ser → era (NOT fue).

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *While I was studying, I was listening to music.*
exercise.hint: Two ongoing past actions → both imperfect. mientras = while.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *It was raining and it was cold.* (llover, hacer frío)
exercise.hint: Weather in the past = imperfect: llover → llovía; hacer → hacía frío.
distractors: Estaba lloviendo y hacía frío ayer.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imperfect of *ver* (yo) — a past habit.
exercise.sentence: De niño, ___ la tele todas las tardes.
exercise.hint: ver keeps its e: ve- + ía → veía.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *When I was young, I was very happy.* (use ser for both)
accept: Cuando era joven, era muy feliz.
cuando era joven, era muy feliz
Cuando era joven era muy feliz.
Cuando era joven, era muy feliz
exercise.hint: Both are lasting states/descriptions → imperfect era (NOT fue).
distractors: Cuando fui joven, fui muy feliz.
Cuando era joven, fui muy feliz.
Cuando era joven, estaba muy feliz para siempre.

exercise.prompt: Match each verb with its yo-form in the imperfect.
Translate: *We used to go to school by bus.* (ir)
accept: Nosotros íbamos al colegio en autobús.
Íbamos a la escuela en autobús.
exercise.hint: 'used to go' = habitual → imperfect of ir: íbamos (nosotros, with accent).

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imperfect of *ser* to tell the time: 'It was three o'clock.'
exercise.hint: Clock time uses ser in the imperfect; 'three' is plural → eran las tres.
